name: XTDB Bitemporal Database API
what_it_does: Open-source bitemporal database with SQL and Datalog API — enabling developers to query historical data at any point in time (valid-time and transaction-time), implement immutable audit logs, and manage temporal data with SQL/Datalog queries, making the past queryable and auditable without separate audit tables.
use_cases: Querying historical states via XTDB SQL for point-in-time data analysis and auditing
Implementing bitemporal data management via XTDB for regulatory compliance audit trails
Building immutable event logs via XTDB for financial transaction history and reconstruction
Querying slowly-changing dimensions via XTDB temporal SQL for data warehouse analytics
Implementing GDPR time-based data access via XTDB for temporal data lifecycle management
not_for: High-throughput OLTP (XTDB is not optimized for heavy transactional workloads)
General-purpose document storage (use MongoDB or PostgreSQL for flexible document storage)
Teams unfamiliar with bitemporal concepts (significant conceptual overhead)
best_when: A team needs to track how data changed over time with both system time (when data was stored) and valid time (when it was true in the world) — especially for financial, regulatory, or audit use cases.
avoid_when: You don't need temporal querying, need high-throughput OLTP, or prefer established enterprise database vendors.

auth.notes: XTDB v2: HTTP REST API (no authentication in Community edition — network-level security required). SQL via HTTP: POST /query with SQL body. Datalog API: POST /query with Datalog EDN body. PostgreSQL wire protocol: XTDB v2 supports PostgreSQL wire protocol — use any PostgreSQL client. JDBC: connect via JDBC for Java/JVM applications. XTDB Cloud: authentication via API key. Self-hosted: no built-in auth — use reverse proxy or VPC isolation.

pricing.paid_tiers: XTDB Cloud: custom enterprise pricing; support contracts available
pricing.notes: UK company (London). Founded by Jeremy Taylor and James Henderson (JUXT team). Apache 2.0 license. XTDB v1 (Clojure-native, Datalog) → v2 (SQL + Datalog, Java/JVM, major rewrite). Bitemporality: two time dimensions — valid-time (business time) + system-time (transaction time). Every row has history — SELECT AS OF SYSTEM_TIME, SELECT AS OF VALID_TIME. SQL:2011 temporal extensions: XTDB implements temporal SQL standard. Datalog API: Clojure-compatible for expressive queries. Append-only: no mutable deletes — corrections are new records. PostgreSQL wire protocol (v2): psql, JDBC, any PG client works. Immutable: past data never changed — GDPR: 'forget' via temporal hiding. Used by: regulatory, financial, and audit-heavy applications.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: NO AUTH in Community edition (network security required). PostgreSQL wire protocol in v2 (use PG clients). SQL + Datalog. Bitemporal concept required. XTDB v1 vs v2 are major API differences. Apache 2.0. UK company. Clojure heritage.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Bitemporal design: all reads are idempotent (immutable history). SQL queries idempotent. INSERT is append-only — idempotent for same valid-time range. No mutable updates — corrections are new temporal records.
known_agent_gotchas: NO AUTHENTICATION in Community edition: add reverse proxy auth or VPC isolation before exposing
XTDB v1 vs v2 are MAJOR differences: v2 uses SQL + PostgreSQL wire protocol; v1 is Datalog-only
PostgreSQL wire protocol (v2): use existing PG JDBC drivers, psql, or any PG-compatible client
Bitemporality: understand valid-time vs system-time distinction before designing schema
Immutable design: no physical deletes — GDPR compliance requires temporal hiding, not deletion
Clojure heritage: Datalog API is expressive but Clojure-centric — SQL API is the v2 primary interface

security_score_components.security_notes: CRITICAL: No built-in authentication in Community edition — add network-level security (reverse proxy, VPC). Apache 2.0: fully auditable codebase. GDPR-friendly design: temporal hiding for data deletion requirements. Immutable history: strong audit trail by design. UK company: GDPR compliance by design philosophy. PostgreSQL wire protocol: TLS available via proxy. XTDB Cloud: adds authentication and TLS. Self-hosted operators: responsible for all access control. JVM security: standard Java security practices apply.
